title: Fix 'Backend connection unavailable' error firing immediately on Publish click

requirements.summary: Replace the current actor readiness guard in StepPublish.tsx with a fully reactive implementation that subscribes to the actor reference, derives an isActorReady boolean, disables the publish button with a spinner/label when the actor is not ready, and performs an explicit null-check at invocation time
acceptanceCriteria: The 'Publish Invitation' button is disabled and shows 'Connecting to backend…' text/spinner from the moment the StepPublish component mounts until the actor reference is confirmed non-null.
Clicking the button while the actor is null does nothing — no error toast, no canister call, no hang.
Once the actor becomes non-null, the button becomes enabled automatically without requiring a page refresh.

If the actor is null at the moment the publish handler fires (race condition), a clear inline error message is displayed ('Backend not ready — please wait a moment and try again') and the canister call is not attempted.
The spinner/disabled state does not flash unnecessarily if the actor initializes quickly — it should resolve smoothly.

file_operations.description: Add a reactive actor readiness check using useActor hook in a useEffect or useMemo that watches the actor value. Derive an isActorReady boolean that is only true when the actor is confirmed non-null. Update the UI to disable the 'Publish Invitation' button and show a 'Connecting to backend…' spinner/label whenever isActorReady is false. In the publish handler (handlePublish), add an explicit null-check on the actor at invocation time and abort with a user-facing inline error message ('Backend not ready — please wait a moment and try again') if it is still null, ensuring no canister call is attempted with a null actor.

requirements.summary: Add a top-level actor readiness guard in CreateInvitePage.tsx handlePublish function that checks the actor is strictly non-null before assembling the InvitePayload, and aborts with a clear inline error message if the actor is null
acceptanceCriteria: handlePublish in CreateInvitePage.tsx returns early with a visible error if the actor is null at call time.
The error message is shown inline (not just in the browser console) so the user knows what happened.
No canister call is attempted when the actor is null.
The normal publish flow is unaffected when the actor is properly initialized.

file_operations.description: In the handlePublish function, add a top-level actor readiness guard that checks the actor instance is strictly non-null before assembling the InvitePayload or making any canister call. If the actor is null at this point, immediately abort the publish flow, display a clear inline error message to the user ('Backend connection unavailable — please wait a moment and try again'), and return early. This guard serves as a second line of defense in addition to the button-level guard in StepPublish.tsx.

requirements.summary: Audit all mutation hooks in useQueries.ts (useCreateInvite, useUpdateInvite) to ensure each mutation function receives the actor as a dependency, performs an explicit non-null assertion on the actor before any canister call, and returns a descriptive error object if the actor is null
acceptanceCriteria: useCreateInvite and useUpdateInvite return a descriptive error (not an unhandled exception) when the actor is null.
The error message clearly states the actor is not yet initialized.
No TypeError or 'Cannot read properties of null' errors appear in the console when publishing.
Mutation hooks work correctly when the actor is properly initialized.

file_operations.description: Audit all mutation hooks (useCreateInvite, useUpdateInvite, and any other hook that calls the actor). Ensure each mutation function: (1) receives the actor as a dependency that is watched reactively, (2) performs an explicit non-null assertion on the actor before any canister call, and (3) throws or returns a descriptive error object (e.g., { error: 'Actor not initialized' }) if the actor is null — rather than throwing a generic JS error or calling a null reference. This ensures the error propagates gracefully to the calling component.
